topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The plastic stick lay on the kitchen counter, a stark, damning piece of evidence. My hands were shaking so badly I almost dropped it. The results blinked back at me: a match. A confirmation. I reread the data, the percentages, the undeniable biological link that stretched back generations. A cold, hard knot tightened in my chest, constricting my breath until it was shallow and painful. I stared at the printout, the vibrant colors of the report seeming to mock the sudden, sickening realization that my entire foundation—the story I’d built of my own lineage—was a carefully constructed lie. I pushed the paper away with a sharp, jerky movement, the sound echoing too loudly in the silent apartment. I needed air, needed to scream, but all that escaped was a dry, rasping sound.

[story 2]

"This is a joke," I spat, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. I paced the length of the living room, my boots clicking a furious rhythm against the hardwood floor. Every surface seemed to hold the answer, every photograph a potential betrayal. The DNA report sat on the coffee table, mocking my composure with its clinical precision. I picked up a nearby vase, my knuckles white as I gripped the ceramic, the vibration of my own agitation running through my arms. Why hadn't they told me? Why the elaborate deception? A wave of intense heat flooded my face, and I squeezed my eyes shut, fighting the urge to smash something. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles screaming beneath my skin.

[story 3]

I sat at my desk, the glow of the monitor reflecting in my wide, unblinking eyes. The sequencing data swam before me, a terrifying tapestry of genetic markers that suddenly redefined my entire existence. A slow, deep tremor started in my core, spreading outward until my legs felt unsteady. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and let out a low, guttural sound that was more animal than human. My breathing hitched, ragged and fast. I slammed my fist onto the desk, the impact rattling the pens and papers. "No," I whispered, the sound barely audible, yet vibrating with a profound sense of wrongness. The silence of the room amplified the furious pounding in my ears.
